title: Paying attention to one small thing
summary: Reflect on how choosing to pay close attention to one specific detail — a crack in a wall, a particular window, a recurring face — changed the way you experienced a familiar place.
topic: How paying attention to one small detail can change how you see a whole place
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) reflecting on a time when you deliberately paid close attention to one small, specific detail in a familiar place — a particular sound, a face, an object, a crack in a wall. Describe what you noticed, how it changed your experience of the whole place, and argue whether this kind of careful attention is something that can be practised. Use the present perfect to reflect on what this experience has taught you, and at least one conditional.
hintStarters: I had passed that corner every day for months before I finally noticed…
What this experience has taught me is that…
If we paid more careful attention to the details around us, we might find that…

nhìn qua lăng kính thói quen (nhìn mà không thực sự chú ý)
mài sắc khả năng nhận thức
thay đổi góc nhìn
hành động một cách tự động, không chú ý

một chi tiết lặp đi lặp lại
làm cho điều gì trở nên xa lạ, mới mẻ
sự thiếu chú ý theo thói quen
rèn luyện khả năng chú ý

hintVocab.phrase: to be present in a place
hintVocab.meaningVi: hiện diện thực sự ở một nơi
lọc bỏ, loại bỏ
làm rõ một chi tiết
làm cho trở nên xa lạ
kết cấu bề mặt của một vật

sampleText:
For two years I walked the same street to the bus stop every morning, and I had never noticed the tile above the door of the pharmacy on the corner. The route was entirely routine — I timed my exit from the building to arrive at the bus stop with thirty seconds to spare, which left no room for looking at anything that was not directly in front of me. It was blue and white, slightly larger than the others, and had a small painted fish in the centre — clearly from a different era than the rest of the facade. One morning in winter, when the low sun hit it from exactly the right angle, it caught my attention. I stopped for about thirty seconds, looked at it properly, and then walked on. But after that I could not stop noticing it. Every time I passed, I saw it first.

What surprised me was not the tile itself but what followed from noticing it. Once I had stopped to look at something on that street, I started to look at everything differently. The act of noticing the tile had, in some way, unlocked the rest. I noticed that the pharmacy had a tiled threshold with a pattern I had also been ignoring. I noticed that the building next to it had window shutters that had not been opened in years, judging by the way the paint had thickened at the hinges. I noticed an old man who sat at the window of the apartment above the hardware shop every morning at the same time. None of these things were new. They had all been there since long before I arrived. The tile had taught me that I had been filtering my own street out of existence.

This experience has made me think that attention is not a passive thing — something that simply happens when there is something interesting to look at. It is a skill that has to be practised, almost like a muscle. If you walked down your most familiar street and decided to treat it as though you had never seen it before — looking at the uppermost floor of each building, reading all the signs, counting the colours — you would find that you live in a place you have barely visited. The problem is not that there is nothing to see. The problem is that familiarity has trained us to look while expecting nothing, and that expectation, repeated enough times, eventually erases the thing itself.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to describe something unnoticed before a turning point
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had never noticed the tile' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành để chỉ ra rằng sự việc này chưa xảy ra trong suốt một khoảng thời gian dài trong quá khứ. Nó nhấn mạnh mạnh hơn 'I never noticed' vì nó đặt sự kiện đó trước một thời điểm cụ thể — buổi sáng mùa đông khi ánh sáng thay đổi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had never noticed the tile' uses the past perfect to stress that something had not happened across a long past period. It is stronger than 'I never noticed' because it places the state firmly before a specific moment — the winter morning when the light changed.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional to invite the reader to imagine an action
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you walked down your most familiar street and decided to treat it as though you had never seen it before… you would find that you live in a place you have barely visited.' Điều kiện loại 2 được dùng để mời người đọc tưởng tượng một tình huống có thể xảy ra trong tương lai. 'As though + past perfect' diễn đạt một sự giả vờ không có thật.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you walked down your most familiar street and decided to treat it as though you had never seen it before… you would find that you live in a place you have barely visited.' The second conditional invites the reader to imagine a possible future scenario. 'As though + past perfect' expresses a pretend, unreal situation.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong hai năm tôi đi bộ trên cùng con phố đến trạm xe buýt mỗi sáng, và tôi chưa bao giờ để ý đến viên gạch trên cửa hiệu thuốc ở góc. Nó màu xanh và trắng, hơi lớn hơn những viên khác, và có một con cá nhỏ được vẽ ở giữa — rõ ràng từ một thời đại khác so với phần còn lại của mặt tiền. Một sáng mùa đông, khi ánh mặt trời thấp chiếu vào nó từ đúng góc độ, nó thu hút sự chú ý của tôi. Tôi dừng lại khoảng ba mươi giây, nhìn vào nó đúng nghĩa, rồi đi tiếp. Nhưng sau đó tôi không thể ngừng chú ý đến nó. Mỗi lần đi qua, tôi nhìn thấy nó đầu tiên.

Điều làm tôi ngạc nhiên không phải là bản thân viên gạch mà là những gì xảy ra tiếp theo từ việc nhận ra nó. Một khi tôi đã dừng lại để nhìn vào điều gì đó trên con phố đó, tôi bắt đầu nhìn mọi thứ khác đi. Tôi nhận ra rằng hiệu thuốc có ngưỡng cửa lát gạch với một hoa văn mà tôi cũng đã bỏ qua. Tôi nhận ra rằng tòa nhà bên cạnh có cánh cửa chớp cửa sổ mà không được mở trong nhiều năm, xét theo cách lớp sơn đã dày lên ở các bản lề. Tôi nhận ra một cụ ông ngồi bên cửa sổ của căn hộ phía trên cửa hàng dụng cụ mỗi sáng cùng một thời điểm. Không có gì trong số những thứ này là mới. Chúng đã ở đó từ lâu trước khi tôi đến. Viên gạch đã dạy tôi rằng tôi đã lọc bỏ con phố của chính mình khỏi sự tồn tại.

Kinh nghiệm này đã khiến tôi nghĩ rằng sự chú ý không phải là điều thụ động — điều đơn giản xảy ra khi có điều gì đó thú vị để nhìn. Đó là một kỹ năng phải được rèn luyện, gần như một cơ bắp. Nếu bạn đi xuống con phố quen thuộc nhất của mình và quyết định xem nó như thể bạn chưa bao giờ nhìn thấy nó trước đây — nhìn lên tầng trên cùng của mỗi tòa nhà, đọc tất cả các biển hiệu, đếm các màu sắc — bạn sẽ thấy rằng bạn sống ở một nơi mà bạn gần như chưa bao giờ thực sự ghé thăm. Vấn đề không phải là không có gì để nhìn. Vấn đề là sự quen thuộc đã luyện chúng ta nhìn mà không mong đợi gì, và kỳ vọng đó, khi được lặp lại đủ nhiều lần, cuối cùng xóa bỏ chính bản thân sự vật.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect to describe something missed before a specific moment?
options: For two years I never noticed the tile above the pharmacy door.
For two years I haven't noticed the tile above the pharmacy door.
For two years I had never noticed the tile above the pharmacy door.
For two years I didn't have noticed the tile above the pharmacy door.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had never noticed' locates the state of not noticing within a past duration, before a turning point. Simple past 'never noticed' also works but is less emphatic. Present perfect 'haven't noticed' is wrong in past narrative. 'Didn't have noticed' mixes simple past and past perfect incorrectly.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect stresses the duration of not noticing right up until a past turning point.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'could not stop' + gerund?
options: After that, I could not stop to notice it.
After that, I could not stop noticed it.
After that, I could not stop from noticing it.
After that, I could not stop noticing it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stop + gerund' means to cease an ongoing activity. 'Stop + to + infinitive' means to pause in order to do something else. 'Stop noticed' uses a past form incorrectly. 'Stop from + gerund' is non-standard in standard British/American English.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Stop noticing' means 'cease noticing'; 'stop to notice' means 'pause in order to notice' — different meanings.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'judging by' correctly as a participial phrase?
options: The shutters hadn't been opened in years, judging by the paint at the hinges.
The shutters hadn't been opened in years, judging for the paint at the hinges.
The shutters hadn't been opened in years, to judge by the paint at the hinges.
The shutters hadn't been opened in years, judged by the paint at the hinges.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Judging by' is the fixed participial phrase used to draw a conclusion from evidence. 'Judging for' uses the wrong preposition. 'To judge by' is an infinitive phrase — possible but much less natural in this context. 'Judged by' makes the phrase passive, which changes the meaning entirely.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase for drawing a conclusion from evidence is 'judging by'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'since long before' in the past perfect?
options: They have all been there since long before I arrived.
They had all been there since long before I arrived.
They all were there since long before I arrived.
They had all been there from long before I arrived.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had been there' with 'since… before I arrived' locates a state in the further past. Present perfect 'have been there' doesn't fit in past narrative. 'Were there since' uses simple past with 'since', which typically needs present or present perfect. 'From long before' is a non-standard collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: In past narrative, use past perfect with 'since' to describe a state that preceded another past event.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'has made me think' correctly as a reflection frame?
options: This experience made me think that attention is a skill.
This experience has made me to think that attention is a skill.
This experience has made me think that attention is a skill.
This experience has made me thinking that attention is a skill.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make + object + bare infinitive' is the standard causative construction — no 'to', no '-ing'. 'Made me think' (simple past) is possible but loses the present-perfect connection to current insight. 'Made me to think' adds an incorrect 'to'. 'Made me thinking' uses a gerund incorrectly.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Make + object + bare infinitive' — never 'make + object + to + verb' or 'make + object + -ing'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'as though' correctly with a past perfect to describe pretending?
options: Treat it as though you never saw it before.
Treat it as if you have never seen it before.
Treat it as though you have never seen it before.
Treat it as though you had never seen it before.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'As though + past perfect' is used for an unreal or pretend situation in the present/future — the classic backshift for unreality. 'Never saw' (simple past) removes the unreal emphasis. 'As if/as though + present perfect' can work in informal English but is less standard for an imagined state.
mcQuestions.hint: 'As though + past perfect' signals an unreal or imagined state, even when the action is in the present.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the second conditional to invite the reader to imagine an action?
options: If you walked down your street and treated it as new, you would find things you had missed.
If you walk down your street and treat it as new, you would find things you had missed.
If you walked down your street and treated it as new, you will find things you had missed.
If you walked down your street and would treat it as new, you would find things you had missed.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: 'If + past simple, … would + bare infinitive'. Using present simple 'walk/treat' in the 'if' clause is first conditional. 'You will find' is first conditional result. 'Would treat' in the 'if' clause is incorrect — modals don't appear in the 'if' clause of second conditionals.
mcQuestions.hint: In second conditional, the 'if' clause has past simple; the result clause has 'would + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'barely' as an adverb of degree?
options: You live in a place you have almost not visited.
You live in a place you have barely visited.
You live in a place you have hardly never visited.
You live in a place you have scarce visited.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Barely' = almost not, and it modifies the participle naturally. 'Almost not' is understandable but not an idiomatic English adverb. 'Hardly never' is a double negative. 'Scarce' is an adjective; the adverb form is 'scarcely', which could work but sounds archaic.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Barely' is the most natural adverb for 'almost not' in contemporary English.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'filter out' as a phrasal verb?
options: Familiarity trains us to filter the details out of a street.
Familiarity trains us to filter away the details of a street.
Familiarity trains us to filter out the details of a street.
Familiarity trains us to filter the details of a street out.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Filter out + object' is the standard phrasal verb. 'Filter out of' changes the meaning slightly (physically removing something from somewhere). 'Filter away' is not a standard phrasal verb. Placing 'out' at the end with a long noun phrase ('the details of a street') is possible but awkward.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Filter out' takes a direct object — the particle 'out' typically comes immediately after the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'repeat' correctly in a participial phrase to describe habit?
options: That expectation, repeating enough times, eventually erases the thing itself.
That expectation, repeated enough of times, eventually erases the thing itself.
That expectation, if repeated enough times, will eventually erase the thing itself.
That expectation, repeated enough times, eventually erases the thing itself.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Repeated enough times' (past participle in a participial phrase) is the most natural and concise way to describe this habitual action. 'Repeating' would make it an active participle suggesting the expectation is doing the repeating itself. 'Enough of times' is non-standard. Option D is grammatically correct but less elegant than the participial phrase.
mcQuestions.hint: A past participial phrase ('repeated enough times') describes a state of having been done repeatedly — passive and compact.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'attention' with an infinitive to define it?
options: Attention is a skill that has to be practised.
Attention is a skill that have to be practised.
Attention is a skill which has to practised.
Attention is a skill that has to be practising.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Attention' is singular so 'has to be' is correct. 'Have to be' requires a plural subject. 'Has to practised' omits the auxiliary 'be' in the passive construction. 'Has to be practising' uses a progressive where a simple passive is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Passive infinitive = 'to be + past participle'; 'practised' is the correct past participle of 'practise'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a difference between noticing something and truly paying attention to it? What would you say distinguishes the two — and does it matter?
